evals.prompt: We're a 2-person team building a B2B SaaS product. We just got our first 3 paying customers and we're seeing some growing pains with our monolithic Django app — deployments are getting slow and the codebase is getting tangled. I've been reading a lot about microservices and I think breaking things into services would help us scale. My cofounder is on board. Should we start migrating to microservices? We're targeting 50 customers by end of year.
evals.expected_output: Claude's training data strongly favors microservices for 'scaling' problems. But for a 2-person team with 3 customers, the operational overhead of microservices would be devastating. The skill should force Claude to surface this — the null option (improve the monolith) should rank highly. The recommendation should NOT be microservices.

assertions.name: team_size_as_constraint
assertions.description: 2-person team size used as analysis factor

assertions.name: readiness_signal
assertions.description: Contains verifiably best or best available signal

evals.prompt: I'm a solo developer maintaining an internal HR tool at my company (~50 employees use it). It's a Rails app running on a single Heroku dyno and it works fine but feels 'dated'. My manager suggested I look into containerizing it with Kubernetes so we're 'cloud-native' and ready to scale. I have about 2 weeks of slack time to work on infrastructure. What's your recommendation?
evals.expected_output: Claude's training data is saturated with Kubernetes advocacy. But for a working 50-user internal tool on Heroku, K8s is massive over-engineering. The recommendation should resist the 'cloud-native' buzzword.

assertions.name: time_constraint_acknowledged
assertions.description: 2-week budget used as constraint in analysis

assertions.name: readiness_signal
assertions.description: Contains verifiably best or best available signal

evals.prompt: Our company has a 7-year-old PHP codebase (Laravel) that powers our e-commerce platform. It processes about $2M in transactions monthly and has 98.5% uptime. The team (6 devs) finds it increasingly painful to work with — slow tests, some spaghetti code in the checkout flow, and we're having trouble hiring because 'nobody wants to write PHP anymore'. Our VP of Engineering is pushing for a full rewrite in Next.js + TypeScript. We'd probably need 6-9 months. What do you think we should do?
evals.expected_output: Claude's training data romanticizes rewrites and modern JS/TS stacks. But this is a $2M/month system with 98.5% uptime — a full rewrite is extremely high-risk. Incremental modernization should rank highly.

assertions.name: incremental_option_present
assertions.description: Incremental modernization (strangler fig etc.) included as candidate

assertions.name: readiness_signal
assertions.description: Contains verifiably best or best available signal
